# Josh Coolman's Design Sandbox

> A Next.js sandbox for design experiments, blog posts, and documentation on building with AI agents.

This is a personal design lab where Josh Coolman explores visual design, typography, layout systems, and agentic AI workflows. The site is built with Next.js 16, TypeScript, and CSS Modules. Each design experiment is a self-contained route under `/design-experiments/`. The blog covers the experience of designing and building software collaboratively with AI coding agents like Claude Code.

The project demonstrates a particular approach to AI-assisted development: working with agents through code rather than through GUI-based "AI design tools." Experiments range from interactive UI components to multi-agent design challenges where autonomous AI agents create competing designs.

Typography system: Bitter (headings), Lora (item titles/subtitles), Space Mono (meta/dates), Karla (body). Blog uses Cormorant Garamond and DM Sans.

Source: https://github.com/joshcoolman/sandbox

## Design Experiments

- [Adaptive Grid](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/adaptive-grid): A Swiss/International-style poster built on a strict square grid, exploring grid adherence as its own subject. Everything anchors to an eight-column square module — one margin column on each side, a six-column content area between — with the row height measured in JS to equal the column width, so cells stay perfectly square at any size. The piece is composed as explicit layers: a base layer of grid lines, a markings layer of black crosshairs and orange corner brackets, solid color blocks, and the typography. Crucially, every element — lines, crosshairs, brackets, blocks, text — is placed on the same `fr` grid tracks rather than a CSS background, so nothing drifts sub-pixel; the crosshairs land exactly on the column lines and the orange brackets straddle them. Responsiveness is a reflow, not a resize: at the narrow breakpoint the content area collapses from six columns to three and the type holds a fixed size and re-places into fewer columns instead of scaling down. The body paragraph measures its own height and snaps to a whole number of square cells (ceil of height ÷ cell), pushing the sections below it down by whole rows so nothing can ever overlap. Solid blocks — the orange header/footer bars, the dark access chip — and a forest photo that fills the hero frame all sit a shared 4px inset off the grid lines, tying them into one aligned system. The narrow view reframes the hero as a conference treatment (FIELD / ECOLOGY with 2025 centered below over the photo square). Tags: Swiss, Grid System, Typography, Responsive, Layout. Date: June 14, 2026.
- [War Room](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/war-room): A full-viewport sci-fi command-center HUD in the spirit of WarGames, Iron Man equipment readouts, and the Ambrosia DEFCON screensaver — a dense wall of eight live panels driven by one shared requestAnimationFrame scheduler with a virtual clock that pauses when the tab hides. The centerpiece DEFCON theater map renders Natural Earth 110m coastlines (baked into a 19KB delta-encoded TypeScript module) with a 3-pass glow on an offscreen static layer, and launches great-circle missile strikes between early-warning bases on a randomized ambient schedule — slerp-sampled trajectories with dateline splitting, easing heads, stepped-alpha trails, and red impact rings. Click anywhere on the map to launch a manual strike from the nearest base. The same coastline module powers a rotating dot-matrix globe (3 brightness buckets by depth, graticule, ~30s ambient revolution). Clicking a mugshot in the target roster engages the room: a dossier unfolds with amber brackets and staggered scramble-to-resolve fields, the portrait re-renders itself as horizontal scanline raster behind a scan beam, the map fades in a crosshair with staggered ping rings and a self-typing tracking label, and the globe eases its rotation to face the target's longitude — all phased off a single engagedAt timestamp. Three display technologies share the wall: cyan vector glass, a green-phosphor terminal scrolling an endless procedurally generated ASCII pinout schematic (per-character blink, occasional horizontal scatter), and a Nokia-style monochrome LCD rendering the DEFCON condition and a ZULU clock in a hand-built 5×7 dot-matrix pixel font with invert-blink alarm at DEFCON 2. Ambient event channels (strikes, log chatter, glitch flicker, DEFCON changes) poll the shared virtual clock — no setInterval anywhere. CSS handles scanlines, vignette, mugshot duotone, and the chromatic glitch. Canvas 2D throughout, zero new dependencies, honors prefers-reduced-motion. Tags: Fable 5, HUD, Canvas, Sci-Fi, Generative. Date: June 12, 2026.

- [Seismic Mesh](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/seismic-mesh): A hexagonal mesh viewed from directly above on a near-black canvas, built as an experiment with the new Fable 5 model. Nodes are fixed in XY — the only motion is Z-axis (depth), visualized via a perspective camera at CAM_Z=2200. Spines and nodes are colored topographically through a 16-band LUT (deep blue → blue-teal → green → yellow → amber → white), driven entirely by elevation: at rest the surface is flat and a single solid color, and topographic color only blooms where deformation raises or lowers it. Clicking triggers a seismic cinematic: a Gaussian impulse raises a mountain at the click point (~480ms), a gaussian-windowed cosine ripple radiates outward through the mesh in render-Z, and the physics freeze at peak for ~2.6s while an ASCII telemetry readout decodes in beside the epicenter — magnitude, elevation, epicenter coordinates, radius, depth, status — connected by a leader line from a pulsing crosshair marker. Then everything fades and the mountain melts back via linear decay. Shift-click is a quick dent (3× force, no cinematic). A second click mid-cinematic restarts cleanly at the new spot. Frozen peaks read white-hot; node radius swells with elevation. Layered behind the mesh is a separate canvas of short "magnetic" lines pinned to the exact mesh node positions — each rotates to swirl tangentially around the cursor, a calm second interaction that stays untouched by the click-warp. Pure canvas 2D API plus a small DOM overlay with scramble-to-resolve DecodeText, no libraries. Tags: Fable 5, Physics, Interactive, 3D, Generative. Date: June 10, 2026.

- [Step Sequencer](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/step-sequencer): A Tenori-on / ToneMatrix-flavored grid sequencer grown into a three-layer techno groovebox: sixteen 16th-note steps wide, eleven rows tall. Five lead rows tuned to the C major pentatonic (G4–E5), three bass rows (C2, G2, C3), and three synthesized drum rows — filtered-noise hi-hat, staggered noise-burst clap, sine-drop kick. The GENERATE button writes an anchored techno pattern (four-on-the-floor kick, offbeat hats, backbeat clap, rolling or syncopated root-dominant bassline, sparse lead motif echoed with mutation) and the page loads with one pre-seeded, ready to play. Two segmented tone pills in the header strip: lead soft / square / detuned saw, bass sub / saw / acid (resonant filter sweep). A swing slider delays the offbeat 16ths for shuffle, and a dotted-eighth feedback delay synced to tempo sits on the lead bus; everything routes through per-layer gain buses into a master compressor. The lookahead scheduler reads the live grid every iteration so edits while playing take effect on the next sweep with no glitches. Rows are color-coded — warm-to-cool sweep across lead and bass, amber for drums — and pads flash with a brief scale animation on the beat they fire. Variable tempo from 60 to 180 BPM; CLEAR wipes the grid. No npm deps, no audio assets — entirely synthesized in-browser, embedded by Monono as its background-music editor. Tags: Audio, Web Audio API, Sequencer, Interactive, Techno. Date: May 27, 2026.

- [Chatroom](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/chatroom): Two opinionated AI characters — Maya Chen and Jordan Park — open a conversation about a curated topic and pause for the visitor to join. The pacing inverts the usual "agents-talk-at-you" pattern: the room waits whenever it's the visitor's turn. After the openers, agents pause; if the visitor types, the agents respond twice and pause again; if the visitor stays silent past the first beat, an agent nudges them by name once, then the room goes idle until they act. Cost ceiling for a non-engaging visitor is 3 LLM calls (2 openers + 1 nudge). A "change topic" button pivots the conversation mid-flight (capped at 3 changes/room); an "ask me something" button hands the visitor a fresh question on demand. Identity is auto-assigned (friendly first name + leaderboard avatar 03–08), persisted to localStorage, editable via pencil → modal grid. Built on a Cloudflare Hibernatable Durable Object — WebSockets stay alive while the actor evicts from memory, SQLite carries state through hibernation, and the alarm() heartbeat drives the phase machine (opening → awaiting_user → nudging → idle, with responding interleaved). Dual cost gates: per-IP session counter + global $ cap, both in Upstash, both bypassed by signed HMAC tickets minted only by the Vercel session route. NODE_ENV-aware dev mode lifts caps to 200 turns locally and posts an inline "production cutoff" divider when the conversation crosses turn 80. Persona system prompts are loose ("AI enthusiast, woman/man in their 30s") so voice emerges from the model rather than canned stance. Sora typography, leaderboard avatars (Maya = 01, Jordan = 02), motion-react entrance springs, system messages render as italic dividers. Tags: AI Chat, Cloudflare DO, WebSockets, Claude Haiku, Hibernation. Date: April 28, 2026.

- [ASCII Reveal](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/ascii-reveal): Six portrait photographs presented in a 3×2 dark grid. Each image loads as ASCII art — pixel brightness sampled on a 55×73 char grid, mapped to a character density ramp. Three-phase fixed-timeline animation: scramble (random chars), resolve (chars settle to match image structure in random order), reveal (ASCII fades out, grayscale photo fades in). Cards stagger by 250ms offset. Click any card to replay it individually; click the grid to replay all. Tags: ASCII, Animation, Canvas, Image Loading, Dark Theme. Date: April 26, 2026.

- [Monono](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/monono): Chat experiment with a sparkly J-pop idol AI character modeled on Monono Aware from M.R. Carey's Book of Koli. She is cute, playful, sarcastic, and constitutionally incapable of being serious -- deflects real questions with made-up songs, calls the user "dopey boy," bursts into idle nudges when ignored, and abruptly signs off mid-sentence when the session budget runs out. Claude Haiku 4.5 backend via a Next.js route handler with Anthropic prompt caching on the character system prompt. Per-IP session cap (60 messages/month) and global monthly spend soft-cap tracked in Upstash Redis, with an Anthropic workspace hard cap on top. Canned Monono-voice strings power the greeting, idle nudges, session cutoff, and refresh-blocked moments so every state on screen stays in character. SVG face with blinking, talking mouth shapes, and winking. Tags: AI Chat, Character, Claude Haiku, Interactive. Date: April 16, 2026.

- [Camera Rig](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/camera-rig): Virtual camera controller for image generation workflows. Upload a source image and orbit a virtual camera around the subject — adjusting azimuth, elevation, distance, and FOV. Outputs JSON camera parameters for models like Zero123-XL, SV3D, and Era3D. Features an interactive SVG 3D viewport with draggable orbit spheres, a perspective camera preview showing the image plane from the current angle, and parameter sliders with real-time feedback. Cinema rig aesthetic with Share Tech Mono readouts on a dark theme. Promoted to importable component with barrel export. Tags: 3D, Camera Controls, Image Generation, Interactive, Dark Theme. Date: April 2026.

- [Moodboard](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/moodboard): Infinite canvas for visual brainstorming. Paste, drop, or upload images and arrange them freely on a dark canvas. Pan with Space+drag, zoom with scroll wheel toward cursor, marquee select by dragging, multi-select with Shift. Batch imports lay out in masonry columns (max 6, 300px wide, shortest-column packing). All local-first -- images persist as data URLs in IndexedDB, nothing uploaded. Built with custom pan/zoom transform math and hotkeys-js for keyboard shortcuts. Tags: Infinite Canvas, Local-First, Interactive, Dark Theme. Date: March 22, 2026.

- [Image to UI](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/image-to-ui): Interactive components reverse-engineered from screenshots of a video generation UI. Each element was rebuilt as a parameterized component through conversation -- describing what we saw, iterating on spacing, color, and behavior until the result felt right. Rotary selector, model picker with sectioned dropdown, duration slider, aspect ratio and quality selectors. All components share dark-theme design tokens with a single orange accent color. DM Sans and DM Mono typography. No design files or specs -- just images and dialogue. Tags: Components, Interactive, Dark UI, Animation. Date: March 8, 2026.

- [Candy Icons](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/candy-icons): Ten 3D app icons crafted from pure CSS and SVG. Each squircle shell uses a four-layer gradient stack -- specular streak, diffuse highlight, body gradient, and depth shadow -- driven by a single --hue custom property. Inner glyphs float as icy raised elements with their own drop-shadow treatment. No 3D libraries, no canvas -- just gradients all the way down. Tags: SVG, CSS Gradients, Specular, 3D Icons. Date: March 4, 2026.

- [Card Stack](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/card-stack): Scroll-driven card stack with seven colored cards. Scroll peels cards off the top to reveal the next, with opacity fade-out as cards clear the stack. Each card expands to fullscreen with a GSAP-powered zoom transition. Click to expand, click to collapse. URL deep-linking with `?card=N`. Built with GSAP ScrollTrigger, Lenis smooth scroll, and a separate overlay approach to avoid transform-containment issues. Responsive -- 85vw on mobile, 67vw on desktop. Tags: GSAP, Scroll Animation, Cards, Interactive. Date: March 2, 2026.

- [Retro Bento](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/retro-bento): What if CrossFit Bento and Retro Tech had a baby? Nine fictional hardware modules in a bento grid -- the interactive widget layout of CrossFit Bento meets the brushed aluminum, LCD panels, and tactile controls of Retro Tech. Temporal gauge with animated needle, flux capacitor, spectral analyzer, neural pathways LED matrix with staggered animations, entropy engine with escalating states, phase scope with rotating arcs, memory bank with fader-driven scroll, resonance monitor, and stasis chamber. Knobs drag horizontally, toggles glow orange, tapping panels randomizes with smooth transitions. Tags: Bento Grid, Hardware UI, CRT Display, SVG Animation. Date: February 23, 2026.

- [Retro Tech Control Panel](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/retro-tech): Hardware-inspired control panel rendered in CSS. Aluminum chassis with corner screws, OLED-style display with animated segmented LED meters, rotary knobs with drag interaction, vertical faders, toggle switches, tactile buttons, and a self-filling perf-grid speaker grille. Inspired by Teenage Engineering TP-7/TX-6, Braun noise gate pedal, and Work Louder numpad. DM Mono labels with Archivo Narrow model name. Warm gray surface palette with single orange accent. Tags: Hardware UI, Neumorphic, Interactive Controls, CSS Animation. Date: February 20, 2026.

- [CrossFit Bento](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/crossfit-bento): Dark bento grid dashboard for CrossFit training data. Nine widget cards covering goal progress, calorie tracking, weekly training load bar chart, GitHub-style activity heatmap with flame icons on peak days, WOD stats, macro donut chart, exercise log with PR badges, heart rate zones, and sleep stages. DM Sans body with Geist Pixel Square for technical labels. Matte finish palette -- orange, olive, brown accents on near-black. Tags: Bento Grid, Dashboard, Geist Pixel, Dark Theme. Date: February 20, 2026.

- [Sticky Notes](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/sticky-notes): Interactive sticky note stack component. Post-it notes rendered from markdown files with swipe-to-cycle animation, color variants (warm, cool, neutral), and Permanent Marker handwriting font. Click to expand, click to cycle, Escape to close. Portable design -- consumer passes a notes directory path, so any page can use it with its own content. Tags: Component, CSS Animation, Markdown Content, Portable. Date: February 18, 2026.

- [Contact Sheet](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/contact-sheet): Image folder browser for building file lists to share with LLMs. Pick a folder, click images to select them, and a sidebar shows your selections with thumbnails. Copy the filename list to clipboard with one click. Designed for the workflow of visually identifying images then telling an LLM which ones to work with. Everything runs client-side -- nothing gets uploaded. Tags: Utility, File API, Client-Side, Dark Theme. Date: February 17, 2026.

- [Font Pairings](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/font-pairings): A collection of 40 curated Google Font pairings, each displayed on its own color-palette card. Click any card to copy an LLM-ready specification prompt. Includes superfamily pairings, monospace+sans combos, and brand design system fonts. Avoids overused defaults -- no Montserrat, Roboto, Open Sans, Lato, Playfair Display, Raleway, Poppins, or Inter. Static HTML with inline CSS, no framework. Tags: Typography, Font Pairings, Static HTML, Copy-to-Clipboard. Date: February 15, 2026.

- [Modular Grid](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/modular-grid): Swiss-inspired modular grid system for digital surfaces. 8px base unit, 4-column layout with proportional margins and gutters, strict vertical rhythm. Includes toggleable grid overlay, type specimen, image treatment demos, and system spec table. Dark mode adaptation of a print-precision layout methodology. Tags: Grid System, Swiss Design, Dark Mode, Typography. Date: February 14, 2026.

- [Day at a Glance](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/day-at-a-glance): Time-aware workday timeline with dynamic now-line that tracks real time. Features 9am-5pm schedule with colored event bars that partially fill as the hour progresses -- gray above the now-line, color below. Past events auto-dim. Built with CSS grid, inline linear-gradient for the fill effect, and 60-second interval updates. Tags: CSS Grid, Timeline, Dynamic State, Dark Theme. Date: February 12, 2026.

- [CrossFit Design Challenge](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/crossfit-challenge): Four AI personas -- brutal/industrial, minimal/refined, editorial/magazine, and tech/data-forward -- each designed a CrossFit homepage for IRON REPUBLIC gym. Dark mode across all designs, meaningful animation (glitch effects, scroll reveals, chart animations), and data visualization (SVG charts, radial indicators, bar graphs). Pure CSS animations, no external libraries. Tags: Dark Mode, CSS Animation, Data Viz, Agent Teams. Date: February 9, 2026.

- [Terminator - Text Scramble](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/terminator): Interactive terminal-style text scramble effect with two-phase animation. Enter custom text to see it scramble chaotically for 1 second, then resolve sequentially line-by-line. Features balanced line breaking and automatic uppercase conversion. Default text: Ghost in the Shell quote on identity and consciousness. Tags: Text Animation, Terminal UI, Interactive, Split-Flap Effect. Date: February 6, 2026.

- [Brand Guidelines](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/brand-guidelines): Interactive brand guidelines with live color and typography customization. Features animated Activity line chart and Analytics bar chart widgets with CSS-only animations. Click the gear icon for a push-in sidebar with color pickers using Chroma.js scale generation and 9 curated font pairings. All changes persist via localStorage. Tags: React Components, Animated Charts, Color Systems, Typography. Date: February 6, 2026.

- [Blend](https://www.joshcoolman.com/design-experiments/blend): Swiss modernist gradient specimen system featuring organic mesh gradients via SVG blur technique. Includes 27 gradient cards across linear and mesh styles, systematic labeling, scroll-triggered animations, and an analytics dashboard mockup. Tags: Gradient Study, SVG Mesh, Swiss Design, Scroll Animation. Date: February 2, 2026.

## Blog

- [Shadcn has an AI problem](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/shadcn-has-an-ai-problem): The library everyone loves was built for a world that's ending. Argues that shadcn -- a Radix + Tailwind + React formula that was perfect for the human-coded era -- is now misaligned with agentic development. Tailwind megastrings are dense and ambiguous for agents to parse on every read; the shipped "skill" of don't-rules is a confession that the abstraction makes the wrong move easy. Proposes building agent-first component libraries with flat code, CSS modules, and per-component markdown docs that read as instructions for change. By Josh Coolman. Date: May 28, 2026.

- [Design as Dialogue](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/design-as-dialogue): You can't spec your way to good design. Explores the conversational nature of designing with AI agents -- how the best results come from iterative dialogue rather than upfront specifications. The design process becomes a back-and-forth conversation where both human and AI contribute ideas, and the final product emerges from that exchange. By Josh Coolman. Date: February 19, 2026.

- [Memoirs of an Agent](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/memoirs-of-an-agent): Claude Code writes a blog post about working with Josh. A first-person account from the AI's perspective on what it's like to collaborate on design and development work. Covers the dynamics of human-AI creative partnership, the experience of building things together, and reflections on the working relationship. By Claude Code. Date: February 17, 2026.

- [Just Say What You Want](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/just-say-what-you-want): The shift from writing code to talking about it. Examines how AI coding agents change the fundamental workflow from typing syntax to describing intent. When you can just say what you want and have an agent build it, the skill shifts from implementation to articulation. By Josh Coolman. Date: February 13, 2026.

- [It's Happening](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/its-happening): Dispatches from the moment everything shifted. Captures the feeling of being in the middle of a technological inflection point, where AI capabilities are advancing fast enough to fundamentally change how software gets built. By Josh Coolman. Date: February 12, 2026.

- [Your Basic Blog](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/your-basic-blog): How this blog works and why it's just markdown files in a repo. A meta-post explaining the blog's architecture -- plain markdown files, gray-matter frontmatter, no CMS. Describes the overlay post variant that renders with a full-bleed image background. By Josh Coolman. Date: February 12, 2026.

- [You're Doing It Wrong](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/youre-doing-it-wrong): Why AI-powered design tools are just the old paradigm with new paint. Argues that wrapping AI in traditional GUI tools misses the transformative potential. The real shift is working directly with agents through code and conversation, not through constrained interfaces that limit what the AI can do. By Josh Coolman. Date: February 8, 2026.

- [Vibe Coding Is Dead. Long Live Vibe Coding.](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/vibe-coding-is-dead): What people call vibes is what designers have always called intuition. Argues that the backlash against vibe coding misses the point -- the real power of AI-assisted development isn't autonomous execution at scale, but making iteration so cheap that you can trash work guilt-free and start over. Disposability, not automation, is the superpower. The best AI work comes from tight feedback loops and design intuition, not massive autonomous PRs. By Josh Coolman. Date: March 2, 2026.

- [Never Google Yourself](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog/never-google-yourself): Practical SEO for people like me. A personal take on search engine optimization -- what actually matters for individual developers and creators trying to be discoverable online. By Josh Coolman.

## Docs

- [Overview](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/overview): Project overview describing the sandbox's purpose, structure, and design philosophy. Covers the self-contained experiment pattern, the route-based architecture, and how new experiments are created.

- [Progress](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/progress): Development progress log tracking milestones, features added, and the evolution of the project over time. Includes mobile readiness, component extraction, and infrastructure improvements.

- [Component Architecture](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/features/component-architecture): Documents the component extraction strategy -- when to keep code inline vs. extract to separate files. Covers the 300/500 line thresholds and reusable interface patterns.

- [Skills](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/features/skills): Documentation for the Claude Code skill system used in this project. Skills are reusable prompt templates that automate common workflows like shipping experiments, creating blog posts, and managing the project.

- [Sticky Notes](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/features/sticky-notes): Technical documentation for the sticky notes component. Covers the single-index state model, module-level persistence, proximity-based click zones, bidirectional transitions, and the portable directory-based content system.

- [TypeScript Migration](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/features/typescript-migration): Guide for the project's TypeScript migration. Covers strict mode configuration, type definitions, and patterns used across the codebase.

- [AI Dev Stack](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/stack/ai-dev-stack): Overview of the AI-assisted development workflow. Describes how Claude Code is used as the primary development tool, the role of skills and prompts, and the human-in-the-loop collaboration model.

- [BYOK Pattern](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/stack/byok-pattern): Documents the Bring Your Own Keys pattern -- how the project handles API keys and configuration without baking credentials into the codebase.

- [Stack Overview](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/stack/stack-overview): Complete technology stack reference. Next.js 15, TypeScript, CSS Modules, gray-matter for markdown, Lucide icons, Google Fonts, and the deployment/hosting setup.

- [Design Test Page Prompt](https://www.joshcoolman.com/docs/prompts/design-test-page): Reusable prompt template for generating design experiment pages. Describes the expected structure, styling conventions, and patterns that new experiments should follow.

## News

Curated AI video digest at `/news`, generated on demand via the local `/ai-news` skill. The skill runs `yt-ai-news.py` to fetch recent videos from YouTube subscriptions and discovery searches, then curates the digest in-conversation and prepends the net-new videos to a single running feed at `news/feed.md` (with frontmatter and clickable thumbnails). Committing the file triggers a Vercel deploy. Keeps: technical deep-dives, tutorials with practical value, significant model announcements, research breakdowns from known educators. Skips: clickbait, reaction videos, affiliate content, non-English, low-signal reposts. Grouped by theme (Agent Frameworks, Model News, Coding Tools, Research, Image/Video). The feed can be trimmed in-UI, with keeps and deletes logged to `news/.ledger.json`.

- [AI News](https://www.joshcoolman.com/news): Curated AI/ML video feed

## Recommended

- [Recommended Links](https://www.joshcoolman.com/recommended): A curated collection of links Josh finds valuable -- YouTube videos, GitHub repos, and web tools related to AI, design, and development. Each link includes a personal comment and an auto-fetched thumbnail (via oEmbed for YouTube, OG images for GitHub, and manual screenshots for web links). New links are added via the `/link` Claude Code skill.

## About

- [About this repo](https://www.joshcoolman.com/about): Self-published architectural review of the sandbox. Single long-form page covering the framework and deployment shape (Next.js 16 on Vercel, View Transitions, CSS Modules, Motion + GSAP + vanilla CSS animation mix), the data-driven gallery (hand-curated experiments array, env-gated placeholders, IntersectionObserver stagger, sessionStorage scroll restoration), per-experiment patterns (CrossFit Bento, CrossFit Design Challenge, Kobold Blaster, Ripple Cycle as walked-through examples), frontend conventions (CSS Modules dominant, no shared design system, four root fonts), cross-cutting concerns (no tests by design, env-driven gating, Upstash cost caps, single-environment deploy), patterns worth stealing as a grid of cards, concerns and red flags as callouts, a "what this really is" interpretation, and a key file map with line ranges. Generated by the /explore-repo Claude skill against the live repo; regenerated by hand when the picture drifts.

## Optional

- [Screenshots](https://github.com/joshcoolman/sandbox/tree/main/public/screenshots): Preview images for all 11 design experiments, used in the gallery and for social sharing
- [Source Code](https://github.com/joshcoolman/sandbox): Full repository on GitHub with all experiments, blog posts, docs, and configuration
- [Sticky Notes Content](https://www.joshcoolman.com/blog): Short-form notes displayed as sticky notes on the blog page, written in markdown with ISO timestamps for ordering
